title: Fascism and Mussolini
description: Questions about Mussolini and Fascism during the 1920s and 1930s

assessment_items.question_content:
True or False:  The term "fascism" was first used to describe Mussolini's party in Italy in the early 1920s.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This is true. The term "fasci" was used to refer to a political or revolutionary group before Mussolini, but the term "fascism" was first used to describe Mussolini's party in Italy in the early 1920s.

choices.content: True
False

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following statements about this symbol are true?

![Image showing a bundle of rods with an axe](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2c06fe8203b30103a85456c4ddc6a9ec.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a bundle of rods called a "fascio" in Italian and "fasces" in Latin and dates to even pre-Roman times to represent authority (it was NOT a negative sign of aggression).  

The figurative term "Fasci" as a group or league comes from this idea of a bundle, it symbolized "strength through unity." 

The Nazis are considered to be an example of a fascist movement, but they didn't consider themselves fascist.

Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/mussolini-fascism/v/fascism-and-mussolini).
The correct three answers are:
- it symbolized "strength through unity" 
- it was the symbol of Mussolini's National Fascist Party
- it is a bundle of rods (with an axe) called a "fascio" in Italian and "fasces" in Latin

choices.content: It was the symbol used by Nazis
It is a bundle of rods (with an axe) called a "fascio" in Italian and "fasces" in Latin
It was considered to be a negative sign of aggression in Roman times
It symbolized "strength through unity"
It was the symbol of Mussolini's National Fascist Party

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following statements are **NOT** true of Benito Mussolini?


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Mussolini did *not* support the Republicans in Spain. In fact, he supported Francisco Franco and the Nationalists against the Republicans in Spain.

The Pact of Steel was *not* the reason Italy entered into WWII (the "Pact of Steel" was a military alliance between Italy and Germany, but Italy waited until June of 1940 to enter on the side of the Axis. WWII had officially started in September 1939 when Germany invaded Poland—although the Japanese and Chinese were already at war by 1937).

These are the only two statements that are **NOT** true of Benito Mussolini.

choices.content: He supported the Republicans against the Nationalist forces of Francisco Franco in the Spanish Civil War.
Because of his "Pact of Steel" with Hitler, he entered Italy into WWII with the Axis as soon as Britain and France declared war on Germany.
He felt that Nazi ideas of racial purity were misguided, but later did collaborate with the Nazis to persecute Jews in Italy.
He was called "Il Duce" or "The Leader."
He had established an absolute fascist dictatorship in Italy by 1926.

assessment_items.question_content:
Which African nation(s) did Italy invade in 1935 to expand its empire?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Eritrea and Eastern Somalia were already Italian possessions.
Djibouti was a French possession (French Somaliland). Kenya and the northern part of Somalia were British possessions. None of these were invaded by Italy.

Mussolini invaded only independent Ethiopia (Abyssinia) in 1935.

Learn more in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/mussolini-fascism/v/mussolini-aligns-with-hitler).

choices.content: Ethiopia
Somalia
Kenya
Eritrea
Djibouti

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false:  Italy's Mussolini was aligned with Hitler as soon as Hitler came to power in 1933.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/mussolini-fascism/v/mussolini-aligns-with-hitler).

Mussolini didn't initially think much of Hitler and his Nazi ideology.  It was only in 1935, when Italy invaded Ethiopia, and Britain and France distanced themselves from Mussolini, that he became more closely aligned with Hitler.

choices.content: True
False

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is true about Mussolini's rise to power?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/mussolini-fascism/v/mussolini-becomes-prime-minister).

There are three correct answers:

Mussolini's rapid rise to power from 1919 to 1922 was based on his bold charisma, a rising discontent with a perceived weak leftist-government, and violent intimidation from the "blackshirts".

The Fascists became a national party in 1921 (which was also when Mussolini was first democratically elected to the Chamber of Deputies).

Mussolini's "March on Rome" in 1922 with over 200,000 followers caused King Victor Emmanuel III to appoint him Prime Minister (an office he held from 1922 to 1943).

The two incorrect answers:

Mussolini's rise predates Hitler's and, in fact, inspired Hitler to attempt his failed "Beer Hall Putsch" in 1923.  So It was NOT inspired by Adolf Hitler's rise to power in Germany.

Mussolini never caused the King to abdicate.

choices.content: It was aided by loosely controlled paramilitary squads called the "blackshirts."
He was able to force the King to abdicate
He was first democratically elected to the Chamber of Deputies (lower house of  the Italian parliament) in 1921.
The King appointed him Prime Minister in 1922.
It was inspired by Adolf Hitler's rise to power in Germany.

assessment_items.question_content:
Which TWO of the following answers are **NOT** true?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/mussolini-fascism/v/fascism-and-mussolini).

Stalin was *not* a typical Fascist leader. Joseph Stalin was a totalitarian dictator of the U.S.S.R. which is a characteristic of fascism, but he was also communist (one could argue that he was the face of communism for much of the early 20th century).  Fascists tend to be fiercely anti-communist.  

The term "fascist" was *not*  first coined as a derogatory term to describe overbearing governments. Even though calling someone a "fascist" today would be considered derogatory, the term was first coined and embraced by Mussolini and his followers to describe themselves (the Fascist Party).

These are the only two statements that are NOT true.

choices.content: The word "fascism" comes from the Italian word "Fasci" which meant "group" or "league."
Adolf Hitler typified a fascist ruler.
Joseph Stalin typified a fascist ruler.
The term "Fascist" was first coined as a derogatory term to describe overbearing governments.
The words "Fascism"  and "Fascist" were first used in Italy in the 1920s to describe Mussolini's party.

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following are associated with fascism (select all that apply)?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/mussolini-fascism/v/fascism-and-mussolini).

Fascist regimes, typified by that of Mussolini's Fascist Party, are aggressively nationalistic.  They also tend to be anti-communist  (and thus are against ideas of class warfare).  Even though Fascist regimes tend to be more associated with the extreme right, they believe that economic interests need to be subordinate to a dominant government (definitely not limited government).  This strong government is often ruled by an absolute dictator (like Mussolini or Hitler).

So, the three answers to this question that are correct are "extreme nationalism," "anti-communist," and "dictatorship."

choices.content: extreme nationalism
class warfare
limited government
anti-communist
dictatorship
